stops.name: Moulin Rouge
stops.shortDescription: Easily identified by the red windmill on its roof, this cabaret is the birthplace of the modern can-can dance. It has been entertaining Parisian society since it opened in 1889.

stops.name: Café des Deux Moulins
stops.shortDescription: A typical Parisian café that gained international fame as the workplace of the title character in the 2001 film 'Amélie'. Its name references the two historical windmills nearby.

stops.name: Le Mur des Je t'aime
stops.shortDescription: A 40-square-meter wall in the Jehan Rictus garden square covered in enameled lava tiles. The phrase 'I love you' is written here 311 times in 250 different languages.

stops.name: Place du Tertre
stops.shortDescription: The vibrant artistic heart of Montmartre, where numerous artists set up their easels daily to paint portraits for tourists. It evokes the village's bohemian past when Picasso and Utrillo lived nearby.

stops.name: Sacré-Cœur Basilica
stops.shortDescription: Perched at the highest point in Paris, this stunning white basilica offers panoramic views of the entire city. It was built between 1875 and 1914 as a national penance following the Franco-Prussian War.

stops.name: Musée de Montmartre
stops.shortDescription: Housed in the oldest buildings of Montmartre, dating back to the 17th century. It features beautiful gardens where Auguste Renoir painted his famous 'La Balançoire' (The Swing).

stops.name: La Maison Rose
stops.shortDescription: A picturesque, pastel-pink house at the corner of a cobbled street that has been operating as a restaurant for over a century. It was frequently painted by Maurice Utrillo and remains a beloved Montmartre landmark.

stops.name: Le Clos Montmartre
stops.shortDescription: The last remaining functioning vineyard in central Paris. Hidden on a steep slope, it produces about 1,500 bottles of wine each year which are auctioned for local charities.

stops.name: Au Lapin Agile
stops.shortDescription: A famous, historic cabaret housed in a tiny pink cottage. In the early 20th century, it was the favorite drinking and meeting spot for bohemian artists like Picasso and Modigliani.

stops.name: Rue de l'Abreuvoir
stops.shortDescription: Often considered one of the prettiest and most photogenic streets in all of Paris, it gently curves down the hill lined with ivy-covered houses.

stops.name: Place Dalida
stops.shortDescription: A picturesque little square dedicated to the famous singer Dalida who lived nearby. It features a bronze bust that visitors traditionally rub for good luck.

stops.name: Le Passe-Muraille
stops.shortDescription: Located in Place Marcel Aymé, this quirky bronze statue depicts a man walking directly out of a stone wall, paying homage to a classic French short story.

stops.name: Moulin de la Galette
stops.shortDescription: One of the only two surviving historical windmills in Montmartre. It was famously painted by Pierre-Auguste Renoir in his masterpiece 'Bal du moulin de la Galette'.

stops.name: Le Bateau-Lavoir
stops.shortDescription: Located in Place Émile Goudeau, this was a famous residence for impoverished artists where Pablo Picasso lived and painted his groundbreaking work 'Les Demoiselles d'Avignon'.

stops.name: Abbesses Metro Station
stops.shortDescription: The tour concludes at this iconic metro station, featuring one of the two remaining original glass-covered Art Nouveau entrances designed by Hector Guimard.
